texts.content: I don't like being treated like a confidence trickster, and I'm sure Tolliver likes it even less. I retreat inside myself, and I don't let them touch my quick. Tolliver tries to do that, too, but he is less successful. He gets very upset when people impugn our honor.

They were shooting accusations back and forth, like I wasn't in the room. I wondered if Tolliver could hear them from the waiting room. "You all want to hash this out while we go back to the hotel?" I asked, cutting through the arguments. They all stopped and looked at me. "I think we brought you here under the wrong impression," Branscom said.
"I just got cold feet, I guess." She tried to look charmingly apologetic, but it didn't work for me.
Paul Edwards, Sybil Teague's attorney, had drawn the short straw. So here he was in my room. The others, I assumed, had scattered to step back into their daily routine.

Tolliver was caught between a rock and a hard place, and I waited to see what he'd do to get out of it. While he squirmed, I looked from young male face to young male face, trying to meet each set of eyes and smile, the squeaky non-sexual smile of a newcaster. Only two of them made the effort to nod to me; the others either evaded my gaze or scowled at me. This was not good.
"Tell me about going out with the tropper," he said. His voice was mild, but I knew that was deceptive. I'd been waiting for that shoe to drop all day.

"Oh, sure, the government just loves me." Paying taxes was excruciating—not because I minded giving the devil his due, but because accounting for my income was very difficult. I called myself a consultant. So far, I'd flown under the radar, but that would change sooner or later.

"He's at the motel where ya'll are at. But don't expect to get much sense out of him. Helen may have quit drinking, but Jay ain't. She had to take out a restraining order on him, I guess a year or two after Sally was born. Jay used to be a nice-looking man, and he had sweet folks, but he ain't worth a tinker's damn."
"You two gonna go watch the sidewalks roll up?" "No, we're going to a gospel singing on the courthouse lawn, evidently."

Tolliver's not quite as omnivorous as I am. He draws the line at romances (he thinks they're too predictable) and spy novels (he finds them ludicruous), but he'll read just about anything else. Westerns, mysteries, science fiction, even some non-fiction—almost any book is grist for our mill.
His dark eyes searched mine. "Dallas is close to Mariella and Grace." "Maybe they won't always feel the same." "Maybe they will. There's no point banging our heads against that wall."
"I thought Helen was a touch nut to crack," I muttered. "I just hadn't met Annie Gibson."
When Teenie got into trouble after that, it was when Del's parents decided to interfere between them, or when Helen was on a tear. If Teenie couldn't go out with Dell, she'd raise hell.
